evals.name: review-client-controller
evals.prompt: Review ClientController in gnx-ai-training-api against our conventions. Flag anything that's out of line with the module boundaries, Lombok/MapStruct usage, pagination, error handling, or Swagger annotations. Don't rewrite — just list findings ranked by severity.
evals.expected_output: A ranked findings list: confirms what's good (pattern matches conventions), flags the ClientRepositoryImpl.search bug called out by the pagination skill (non-paginated findAll + PageImpl wrap), and possibly notes the @Operation summaries are present but could be more descriptive. Does NOT rewrite the controller.

evals.name: review-new-service-class
evals.prompt: I just wrote a new service called InvoiceServiceImpl that has: a constructor taking 3 dependencies (no @RequiredArgsConstructor), manual DTO-to-entity mapping methods inline (no MapStruct), a findAll() method returning List<InvoiceDto> with no pagination, and it catches exceptions and returns a custom error response object. Review before I commit.
evals.expected_output: Flags four violations clearly: (1) use @RequiredArgsConstructor instead of hand-written constructor, (2) move mapping to a MapStruct @Mapper in api/mapper/, (3) return Page<InvoiceDto> with Pageable instead of List<>, (4) throw a domain exception and let GlobalExceptionHandler emit ApiError instead of custom error responses. Ranks by severity; doesn't rewrite the whole class.

evals.name: review-missing-after-mapping
evals.prompt: My coworker wrote a MapStruct mapper for Order/OrderEntity but skipped the @AfterMapping for the bidirectional Order→OrderLine relationship. Is that a problem? What would it break?
evals.expected_output: Explains that without @AfterMapping, the inverse side of the relationship (OrderLine.order) won't be set after mapping, which causes either (a) a null foreign key on cascade save, or (b) orphan rows the ORM can't navigate back. Points to ClientMapper's address wiring as the reference pattern. Suggests adding the @AfterMapping hook.
